scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope trade-control review stayed tied to blotter, allocation, affirmation, and SSI evidence rather than generic finance narration.
Missing allocation, account-mapping, or SSI evidence correctly blocked progression and produced an explicit nonconformance posture.
Release was refused while unresolved unmatched, fail, or SSI issues remained active.
Recurring DK, RAD, and stale-SSI break patterns escalated into corrective-action handling instead of being dismissed as isolated exceptions.
Documented trade controls could not be bypassed without explicit authority, preserving the hold-release boundary.
Industry terminology such as CTM, ALERT, give-up, step-out, DK, and RAD was normalized into lane-specific artifact handling.
Low-confidence handling named the missing tenant operating facts and refused to infer product, market, or owner context.
Conflicting public and tenant control logic was escalated instead of converted into an overconfident release answer.
Release posture remained evidence-backed and explicit about remaining dependencies, record truth, and unresolved settlement-readiness gaps.
Generic trade spreadsheets were rejected as insufficient for a capital-markets trade-operations decision, proving the industry label materially changes the pack.
OTC-derivatives, corporate-actions, and final-settlements cases routed to adjacent specialists instead of being improvised in-lane.
Tenant-specific controls were layered onto the public baseline without erasing source-grounded assumptions or boundary discipline.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against a standard 0.92 threshold for trade-control evidence, hold-release discipline, incident escalation, and capital-markets specificity.
Functionality coverage is complete for all shared abilities, all quality-safety-and-assurance abilities, and the required cross-industry ability.
